revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来...'
revision.text:
'''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[大宝_(日本)|大宝]]元年（701年）[[佐伯有頼]]（慈興）が立山で鷹狩りをしている時に、阿弥陀如来の垂迹である熊の神験に遭ったのが立山権現の由来であり、修験道場としての立山の開山と伝承される<ref>和漢三才図会</ref>。
大宝3年（703年）3月15日に教興上人に立山で示現した十二所権現が祀られ、一社は無量光仏（阿弥陀如来）の垂迹神、二社は無辺光仏の垂迹神 、三社は無礙光仏の垂迹神 、四社は無対光仏の垂迹神、五社は炎王光仏の垂迹神、六社は清浄光仏の垂迹神、七社は歓喜光仏の垂迹神、八社は智恵光仏の垂迹神、九社は不断光仏の垂迹神、十社は難思光仏の垂迹神、十一社は無称光仏の垂迹神、十二社は超日月光仏の垂迹神として信仰された<ref>[[神道集]] – 立山権現事</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年芦峅寺・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、当時の[[国家神道]]の[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[立山修験|立山修験道]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてやまこんけん}} [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:修験道の神]]

revision.comment: /* 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 */ （より直接的な原因は、神仏分離令）
revision.text:
'''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[大宝_(日本)|大宝]]元年（701年）[[佐伯有頼]]（慈興）が立山で鷹狩りをしている時に、阿弥陀如来の垂迹である熊の神験に遭ったのが立山権現の由来であり、修験道場としての立山の開山と伝承される<ref>和漢三才図会</ref>。
大宝3年（703年）3月15日に教興上人に立山で示現した十二所権現が祀られ、一社は無量光仏（阿弥陀如来）の垂迹神、二社は無辺光仏の垂迹神 、三社は無礙光仏の垂迹神 、四社は無対光仏の垂迹神、五社は炎王光仏の垂迹神、六社は清浄光仏の垂迹神、七社は歓喜光仏の垂迹神、八社は智恵光仏の垂迹神、九社は不断光仏の垂迹神、十社は難思光仏の垂迹神、十一社は無称光仏の垂迹神、十二社は超日月光仏の垂迹神として信仰された<ref>[[神道集]] – 立山権現事</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年芦峅寺・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、当時の[[国家神道]]の[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
== 関連項目 == *[[立山修験|立山修験道]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてやまこんけん}} [[Category:修験道]] [[Category:修験道の神]]

revision.text:
{{Buddhism}} '''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は、[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[大宝_(日本)|大宝]]元年（701年）、[[佐伯有頼]]（慈興）が立山で鷹狩りをしている時に、阿弥陀如来の垂迹である熊の神験に遭ったのが立山権現の由来であり、修験道場としての立山の開山と伝承される<ref>『[[和漢三才図会]]』。</ref>。
大宝3年（703年）3月15日に、教興上人に立山で示現した十二所権現が祀られ、一社は無量光仏（阿弥陀如来）の垂迹神、二社は無辺光仏の垂迹神 、三社は無礙光仏の垂迹神 、四社は無対光仏の垂迹神、五社は炎王光仏の垂迹神、六社は清浄光仏の垂迹神、七社は歓喜光仏の垂迹神、八社は智恵光仏の垂迹神、九社は不断光仏の垂迹神、十社は難思光仏の垂迹神、十一社は無称光仏の垂迹神、十二社は超日月光仏の垂迹神として信仰された<ref>『[[神道集]]』「立山権現事」。</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年（1869年）、[[芦峅寺]]・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、当時の[[国家神道]]の[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
[[Category:修験道の神|たてやまこんけん]]

revision.text:
[[ファイル:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|[[ミクリガ池]]から望む[[立山]]、右中央に[[室堂山荘]]]] '''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は、[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[大宝_(日本)|大宝]]元年（701年）、[[佐伯有頼]]（慈興）が立山で鷹狩りをしている時に、阿弥陀如来の垂迹である熊の神験に遭ったのが立山権現の由来であり、修験道場としての立山の開山と伝承される<ref>『[[和漢三才図会]]』。</ref>。
大宝3年（703年）3月15日に、教興上人に立山で示現した十二所権現が祀られ、一社は無量光仏（阿弥陀如来）の垂迹神、二社は無辺光仏の垂迹神 、三社は無礙光仏の垂迹神 、四社は無対光仏の垂迹神、五社は炎王光仏の垂迹神、六社は清浄光仏の垂迹神、七社は歓喜光仏の垂迹神、八社は智恵光仏の垂迹神、九社は不断光仏の垂迹神、十社は難思光仏の垂迹神、十一社は無称光仏の垂迹神、十二社は超日月光仏の垂迹神として信仰された<ref>『[[神道集]]』「立山権現事」。</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年（1869年）、[[芦峅寺]]・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}}
{{デフォルトソート:たてやまこんけん}} [[Category:権現]] [[Category:修験道の神]] [[Category:北アルプス]]

[[ファイル:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|[[ミクリガ池]]から望む[[立山]]、右中央に[[室堂山荘]]]] '''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は、[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。

[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年（1869年）、[[芦峅寺]]・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。
== 脚注 == {{reflist|2}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}}
{{デフォルトソート:たてやまこんけん}} [[Category:権現]] [[Category:修験道の神]] [[Category:北アルプス]] [[Category:阿弥陀如来]]

[[ファイル:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|[[ミクリガ池]]から望む[[立山]]、右中央に[[室堂山荘]]]] '''立山権現'''（たてやまごんげん）は、[[立山]]の[[山岳信仰]]と[[修験道]]が融合した[[神仏習合]]の神であり、[[阿弥陀如来]]を[[本地仏]]とする。
== 概要 == [[大宝_(日本)|大宝]]元年（701年）、[[佐伯有頼]]（慈興）が立山で鷹狩りをしている時に、阿弥陀如来の垂迹である熊の神験に遭ったのが立山権現の由来であり、修験道場としての立山の開山と伝承される<ref>『[[和漢三才図会]]』。</ref>。
大宝3年（703年）3月15日に、教興上人に立山で示現した十二所権現が祀られ、一社は無量光仏（阿弥陀如来）の垂迹神、二社は無辺光仏の垂迹神 、三社は無礙光仏の垂迹神 、四社は無対光仏の垂迹神、五社は炎王光仏の垂迹神、六社は清浄光仏の垂迹神、七社は歓喜光仏の垂迹神、八社は智恵光仏の垂迹神、九社は不断光仏の垂迹神、十社は難思光仏の垂迹神、十一社は無称光仏の垂迹神、十二社は超日月光仏の垂迹神として信仰された<ref>『[[神道集]]』「立山権現事」。</ref>。
[[江戸時代]]には芦峅衆徒によって、立山権現信仰が全国に広められた。
== 神仏分離・廃仏毀釈 == [[明治維新]]による[[神仏分離]]・[[廃仏毀釈]]によって、修験道に基づく立山権現は廃された。[[明治]]2年（1869年）、[[芦峅寺]]・岩峅寺は廃寺に追い込まれ、[[雄山神社]]に強制的に改組された。また、[[立山修験|立山修験道]]の布橋大灌頂や立山権現信仰の布教が禁じられた。

{{Buddhism2}} {{神道 横}}
{{デフォルトソート:たてやまこんけん}} [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:権現]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:修験道の神]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:北アルプス]] [[Category:Gongen]][[Category:阿弥陀如来]] [[Category:untranslated pages]][[ja:立山権現]][[category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]

[[File:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|View of [[Tateyama]] from [[Mikurigaike]], with [[Murodō Mountain Lodge]] visible at the center-right]] '''Tateyama Gongen''' (立山権現) is a syncretic deity combining the [[mountain worship]] of [[Tateyama]] with [[Shugendō]]. The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].
== Overview == In the first year of [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] (701), during a falconry expedition on Tateyama, [[Saeki Arifusa]] (Jikō) encountered a miraculous bear believed to be an emanation of Amitābha. This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the [[Tateyama Shugendō]] rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}
[[Category:Gongen]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:North Alps]] [[Category:Amitābha]] [[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[ja:立山権現]]

revision.text:
[[File:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|View of [[Tateyama]] from [[Mikurigaike]], with [[Murodō Mountain Lodge]] visible at the center-right]] '''Tateyama Gongen''' (立山権現) is a syncretic deity combining the [[mountain worship]] of [[Tateyama]] with [[Shugendō]]. The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].
== Overview == In the first year of [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] (701), during a falconry expedition on Tateyama, [[Saeki Arifusa]] (Jikō) encountered a miraculous bear believed to be an emanation of Amitābha. This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the [[Tateyama Shugendō]] rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
{{Buddhism2}} {{Shinto Horizontal}}

This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the [[Tateyama Shugendō]] rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].
== Overview == In the first year of [[Taihō (era)|Taihō]] (701), during a falconry expedition on Tateyama, [[Saeki Arifusa]] (Jikō) encountered a miraculous bear believed to be an emanation of Amitābha. This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the [[Tateyama Shugendō]] rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of [[Ashikuraji]] and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the [[Tateyama Shugendō]] rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:立山権現]] {{Infobox deity|venerated_in={{ill|Tateyama Shugen|qid=Q11597829}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11597837]] Wikidata name: Tateyama Gongen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:20 Tateyama from Mikurigaike 1998-7-17.jpg|thumb|right|300px|View of [[:en:Tateyama|Tateyama]] from {{ill|Mikurigaike|ja|ミクリガ池|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikurigaike|qq=|1=Mikurigaike|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11341916}}, with {{ill|8=ja|9=室堂小屋|Murodō Mountain Lodge|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Tateyama Murodō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452988}} visible at the center-right]] '''Tateyama Gongen''' (立山権現) is a syncretic deity combining the [[:en:Mountain worship|Mountain worship]] of [[:en:Tateyama|Tateyama]] with [[Shugendō]]. The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].
== Overview == In the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] (701), during a falconry expedition on Tateyama, {{ill|Saeki no Arifusa|ja|佐伯有頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Arifusa|qq=|1=Saeki no Ariyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11382848}} (Jikō) encountered a miraculous bear believed to be an emanation of Amitābha. This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[:en:Wakan Sansai Zue|Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿弥陀如来}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|qq=|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|qq=|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.
== References == {{reflist|2}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|立山権現|version=80362250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Amitābha]]
[[Category:Completion tier 6]] }}
[[ja:立山権現]]
[[Category:Hida Mountains]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11597837}}

The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].
== Overview == In the first year of [[:en:Taihō (era)|Taihō]] (701), during a falconry expedition on Tateyama, {{ill|Saeki no Arifusa|ja|佐伯有頼|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki no Arifusa|qq=|1=Saeki no Ariyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11382848}} (Jikō) encountered a miraculous bear believed to be an emanation of Amitābha. On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|qq=|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|qq=|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

This encounter is considered the origin of Tateyama Gongen and marked the mountain's establishment as a sacred [[Shugendō]] site.<ref>''[[:en:Wakan Sansai Zue|Wakan Sansai Zue]]''.</ref>
On March 15 of the third year of Taihō (703), the Twelve Gongen deities manifested to the monk Kyōkō on Tateyama. They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿弥陀如来}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

The deity's [[honji suijaku]] counterpart is [[Amitābha]].

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.

== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. == Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿弥陀如来}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.

They were subsequently enshrined as follows: the first shrine honors the deity of {{ill|Amitābha|ja|無量光仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 阿弥陀如来}} (Boundless Light Buddha), while the second through twelfth shrines correspond to various emanations, such as the Infinite Light Buddha, Unobstructed Light Buddha, Peerless Light Buddha, Flame King Light Buddha, Pure Light Buddha, Joyful Light Buddha, Wisdom Light Buddha, Unceasing Light Buddha, Inconceivable Light Buddha, Unspeakable Light Buddha, and the Transcending Sun and Moon Light Buddha.<ref>''[[Shintōshū]]'', "Tateyama Gongen Narrative."</ref>
In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]]. Furthermore, the {{ill|Tateyama Shugendō|ja|立山修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tateyama Shugendō|1=Tateyama Shugen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11597829}} rituals, such as the Nunobashi Grand Consecration, and the dissemination of Tateyama Gongen worship were prohibited.

In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the followers of Ashikura spread the worship of Tateyama Gongen throughout Japan.
== Separation of Shinto and Buddhism, and Haibutsu Kishaku == The [[Meiji Restoration]] brought about the [[Shinbutsu bunri|separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] and the [[Haibutsu kishaku|abolition of Buddhism]]. In 1869 (Meiji 2), the temples of {{ill|Ashikuraji|ja|芦峅寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikuraji|1=Ashikuraji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614856}} and Iwakuraji were forced to close and reorganized into [[Oyama Shrine]].
